title: Hypertrophic Cardiomyopathy
oneLiner: Inherited heart muscle disease causing asymmetric left ventricular hypertrophy, diastolic dysfunction, and risk of sudden cardiac death (SCD); commonest genetic cardiac disorder.

sections.Killers First — What Will Kill Them In T:
- **Sudden cardiac death (SCD) due to ventricular arrhythmia** — patient collapses, pulseless, shocked rhythm on ECG. *The single finding:* loss of consciousness + documented VT/VF (or unexplained PEA) — treat as per ALS immediately.
- **Left ventricular outflow tract (LVOT) obstruction with hypotension** — syncope, severe chest pain, low BP, harsh systolic murmur at left sternal edge. *The single finding:* dynamic murmur that increases with Valsalva / standing and decreases with squatting; immediate echocardiography needed to confirm gradient.
sections.Presentation:
- **Cardinal symptoms:** exertional dyspnoea (most common), chest pain (angina-like but without epicardial CAD), presyncope/syncope (often exertional), palpitations, fatigue
- **Key exam findings:** 
  - Harsh systolic ejection murmur at left sternal edge ± apex; increases with Valsalva (↓ preload), decreases with squatting (↑ preload)
  - Sustained apex beat, palpable S4 gallop
  - Bifid (double-peak) carotid upstroke (if LVOT obstruction)
  - Look for signs of heart failure (raised JVP, crackles, peripheral oedema) — usually mild unless end-stage
sections.Differential Diagnosis:
- **Aortic stenosis (AS)** — murmur is right 2nd ICS, radiates to carotids, carotid upstroke is slow-rising (not bifid); ECG shows LVH, echo confirms valve area <1.0 cm²
- **Hypertensive heart disease** — concentric LVH, no LVOT gradient, no family history
- **Athlete's heart** — symmetric LVH with normal diastolic function, regression with deconditioning
- **Cardiac amyloidosis** — thick LV walls but low voltage on ECG, right heart failure, extra-cardiac features (macroglossia, carpal tunnel)
- **Fabry disease** — X-linked, LVH + angiokeratomas, corneal opacities, chronic neuropathic pain
- **Splitter:** The single bedside test to differentiate HCM from aortic stenosis is the **response of the murmur to Valsalva** (↑ in HCM, no change or ↓ in AS) — use with caution in elderly / frail patients. Formal differentiation is echo.
sections.Investigations:
- **Bedside:** 12-lead ECG — LVH (voltage criteria + deep septal Q waves), T-wave inversion, left atrial enlargement, ± atrial fibrillation; must document rhythm before any treatment.
- **Bloods:** FBC, U&E, LFT, TFT, BNP/NT-proBNP (elevated in heart failure), troponin (if chest pain — may be mildly raised but rules out infarction), genetic testing (if confirmed).
- **Imaging:**
  - First-line: **Transthoracic echocardiography** — septum to posterior wall ratio >1.3 in diastole, systolic anterior motion (SAM) of mitral valve, LVOT gradient >30 mmHg at rest or with provocation
  - Second-line: **Cardiac MRI** (gold standard) — best for wall thickness quantification, fibrosis (late gadolinium enhancement), and differentiation from other causes of LVH
- **Special tests:** Exercise test (to document LVOT gradient during exertion), 24-hour Holter monitor (for non-sustained VT), genetic counselling and cascade screening of first-degree relatives
- **Diagnostic criteria:** **ESC 2024 (or NICE)** — echocardiographic LV wall thickness ≥15 mm in one or more LV segments that is not explained by loading conditions; plus exclusion of other causes (aortic stenosis, hypertension, athlete’s heart). A positive genetic test confirms.
- **Never-miss test:** **12-lead ECG** in any young patient with syncope or family history of sudden death — a normal ECG almost excludes HCM (sensitivity >95% in young adults); if abnormal, proceed to echo.
sections.Management:
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - If hypotensive **and** known HCM with LVOT obstruction: **give IV phenylephrine** (pure alpha-agonist) to raise BP without increasing contractility — do NOT give inotropes (dobutamine, adrenaline) as they worsen obstruction
  - If chest pain / dyspnoea: sit patient up, give oxygen if SpO₂ <94%, obtain IV access, ECG; avoid nitrates (reduce preload and may provoke obstruction)
  - If sustained VT or VF: synchronised cardioversion / defibrillation per ALS algorithm; amiodarone 150 mg IV over 10 min (if shocked rhythm)
- **Ward (first 24-72h):**
  - Start oral beta-blocker (e.g. bisoprolol 1.25 mg OD, titrate to heart rate 50-70 bpm) to reduce myocardial oxygen demand and LVOT gradient
  - If beta-blocker contraindicated or ineffective: verapamil SR (120–240 mg OD) — but avoid in patients with severe LVOT obstruction or heart failure with reduced EF
  - Monitor for arrhythmias (ECG telemetry), fluid balance (aim euvolemia)
  - Arrange echocardiogram within 24-48h if not done in ED
  - Review for SCD risk assessment using **HCM Risk-SCD calculator** (ESC) — requires age, septal wall thickness, left atrial size, LVOT gradient, NSVT on Holter, family history of SCD, unexplained syncope
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - First-line: beta-blocker (bisoprolol/propranolol) tolerated dose
  - If symptoms persist despite beta-blocker: add disopyramide (250–500 mg TDS) — but only under specialist supervision due to pro-arrhythmic risk
  - If marked LVOT obstruction and drug-refractory: consider septal reduction therapy (alcohol septal ablation or myectomy) — refer to specialist centre
  - All patients with HCM should have **lifestyle advice**: avoid competitive sports, high-intensity exercise, dehydration, alcohol excess
  - Evaluate for ICD if HCM Risk-SCD >6% over 5 years (ESC) or >4% (NICE TG6)
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - Fluids: of caution; avoid aggressive IV fluids unless clearly hypovolaemic (can worsen obstruction if preload is too high? Actually, hypovolaemia is worse. In HCM, maintain euvolemia; if dry, give 250–500 mL bolus of 0.9% saline slowly. If wet, restrict. **Write:** "Maintain euvolemia. Give 500 mL 0.9% NaCl IV over 1h if systolic BP <90 or signs of dehydration; otherwise monitor strict I/O."
  - VTE prophylaxis: low-molecular-weight heparin (enoxaparin 40 mg SC OD) if admitted >24h and high risk (immobility + obesity) — adjust for renal function
  - Diet: normal, but avoid large meals (can reduce preload)
  - Observations: 4-hourly NEWS2, continuous ECG telemetry if admitted for syncope/arrhythmia
sections.Drugs & Doses:
- **Beta-blockers (first-line):**
  - Bisoprolol: start 1.25 mg PO OD, titrate up to 10 mg OD as tolerated (target HR 50-70 bpm)
  - Propranolol: 20–40 mg PO TDS, titrate to 320 mg/day in divided doses if needed
- **Non-dihydropyridine calcium channel blocker (second-line):**
  - Verapamil SR: start 120 mg PO OD, increase to 240–480 mg OD in divided doses if needed — **contraindicated if LVOT gradient >50 mmHg or EF <50%**
- **Disopyramide:** 250 mg PO TDS (max 500 mg TDS) — note: anticholinergic effects (dry mouth, urinary retention); contraindicated in glaucoma, prostatism; must have baseline ECG (prolongs QT)
- **For hypotension in acute LVOT obstruction:**
  - Phenylephrine: 100–200 mcg IV bolus, then infusion 50–200 mcg/min — titrate to systolic BP >90
  - **Do NOT use** dobutamine, adrenaline, milrinone, noradrenaline (increase contractility → worsen gradient)
- **For acute arrhythmia:**
  - Amiodarone: 150 mg IV over 10 min (for sustained VT/VF), then 300 mg IV over 1h (if recurrent)
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:** 
  - Bisoprolol: adjust in severe renal failure (eGFR <20 — start 1.25 mg OD, max 5 mg)
  - Verapamil: use with caution in hepatic impairment (reduce dose by 50%)
  - Disopyramide: dose reduction in renal failure (interval prolongation)
- **Most dangerous dose error:** **Giving dobutamine or adrenaline for hypotension in a patient with LVOT obstruction** — can cause cardiovascular collapse. Always ask "Does this patient have HCM?" before giving any IV inotrope.
sections.Complications:
- **Acute:** 
  - Ventricular arrhythmia (VT/VF) → SCD
  - LVOT obstruction → syncope, acute pulmonary oedema
  - Atrial fibrillation → haemodynamic instability, stroke
- **Chronic:** 
  - Diastolic heart failure (with preserved EF)
  - Mitral regurgitation secondary to SAM
  - Left atrial enlargement → atrial fibrillation → stroke risk (CHA₂DS₂-VASc ≥2 → anticoagulate)
  - End-stage systolic heart failure (≤5% of patients)
  - Infective endocarditis (rare, but consider if LVOT gradient fixed)
sections.Prognosis: - Early diagnosis (symptomatic or high-risk) → annual mortality 1% (with modern management). However, SCD risk is highest in young, symptomatic patients with massive hypertrophy (>30 mm) or NSVT. Many patients live normal lives with symptom control; a minority progress to end-stage heart failure requiring transplant.
sections.Pearls (positive actions):
- **Always perform a Valsalva manoeuvre at the bedside** — if you hear an increase in the murmur, think HCM.
- **Send first-degree relatives for echocardiography screening** (cascade screening) — 50% will have a normal echo at first; repeat every 3-5 years.
- **Use the HCM Risk-SCD calculator** before discharging any newly diagnosed patient — if score >6% (ESC), discuss ICD.
sections.Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid):
- **Do NOT give nitrates** (GTN spray, IV nitroglycerin) for chest pain in suspected HCM — they reduce preload, worsen obstruction, and can cause syncope.
- **Do NOT use verapamil** in patients with LVOT gradient >50 mmHg or reduced EF — it can precipitate heart failure.
- **Do not ignore atrial fibrillation** — patients with HCM need lifelong anticoagulation if CHA₂DS₂-VASc ≥2, even if low score, because stroke risk is higher than in general population.
sections.Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home):
- **Return if:** 
  - Recurrent syncope or near-syncope (especially with exertion)
  - Sustained palpitations (rapid, irregular) — may be atrial fibrillation
  - New chest pain, breathlessness at rest, or leg swelling
- **Follow-up:** 
  - Cardiology clinic within 2-4 weeks for echo review and risk assessment
  - Family screening referral to clinical genetics
  - If ICD implanted: device clinic in 1 month
sections.Escalation Criteria:
- **Call senior / cardiology reg immediately if:**
  - Systolic BP <90 with syncope or severe symptoms (suspect acute LVOT obstruction or VT) — give phenylephrine as above
  - Sustained VT or VF (call crash team)
  - NEWS2 ≥5 with respiratory distress (possible acute pulmonary oedema)
  - GCS <15 or seizure (syncope gone bad)
- **ICU admission:** 
  - Refractory hypotension despite phenylephrine
  - Recurrent ventricular arrhythmias
  - Need for invasive ventilation or haemodynamic support (IABP may be considered, but rarely needed)

rawMarkdown:
## Hypertrophic Cardiomyopathy
**One-liner:** Inherited heart muscle disease causing asymmetric left ventricular hypertrophy, diastolic dysfunction, and risk of sudden cardiac death (SCD); commonest genetic cardiac disorder.

### Killers First — What Will Kill Them In The First Hour?
- **Sudden cardiac death (SCD) due to ventricular arrhythmia** — patient collapses, pulseless, shocked rhythm on ECG. *The single finding:* loss of consciousness + documented VT/VF (or unexplained PEA) — treat as per ALS immediately.
- **Left ventricular outflow tract (LVOT) obstruction with hypotension** — syncope, severe chest pain, low BP, harsh systolic murmur at left sternal edge. *The single finding:* dynamic murmur that increases with Valsalva / standing and decreases with squatting; immediate echocardiography needed to confirm gradient.

### Presentation
- **Cardinal symptoms:** exertional dyspnoea (most common), chest pain (angina-like but without epicardial CAD), presyncope/syncope (often exertional), palpitations, fatigue
- **Key exam findings:** 
  - Harsh systolic ejection murmur at left sternal edge ± apex; increases with Valsalva (↓ preload), decreases with squatting (↑ preload)
  - Sustained apex beat, palpable S4 gallop
  - Bifid (double-peak) carotid upstroke (if LVOT obstruction)
  - Look for signs of heart failure (raised JVP, crackles, peripheral oedema) — usually mild unless end-stage

### Differential Diagnosis
- **Aortic stenosis (AS)** — murmur is right 2nd ICS, radiates to carotids, carotid upstroke is slow-rising (not bifid); ECG shows LVH, echo confirms valve area <1.0 cm²
- **Hypertensive heart disease** — concentric LVH, no LVOT gradient, no family history
- **Athlete's heart** — symmetric LVH with normal diastolic function, regression with deconditioning
- **Cardiac amyloidosis** — thick LV walls but low voltage on ECG, right heart failure, extra-cardiac features (macroglossia, carpal tunnel)
- **Fabry disease** — X-linked, LVH + angiokeratomas, corneal opacities, chronic neuropathic pain
- **Splitter:** The single bedside test to differentiate HCM from aortic stenosis is the **response of the murmur to Valsalva** (↑ in HCM, no change or ↓ in AS) — use with caution in elderly / frail patients. Formal differentiation is echo.

### Investigations
- **Bedside:** 12-lead ECG — LVH (voltage criteria + deep septal Q waves), T-wave inversion, left atrial enlargement, ± atrial fibrillation; must document rhythm before any treatment.
- **Bloods:** FBC, U&E, LFT, TFT, BNP/NT-proBNP (elevated in heart failure), troponin (if chest pain — may be mildly raised but rules out infarction), genetic testing (if confirmed).
- **Imaging:**
  - First-line: **Transthoracic echocardiography** — septum to posterior wall ratio >1.3 in diastole, systolic anterior motion (SAM) of mitral valve, LVOT gradient >30 mmHg at rest or with provocation
  - Second-line: **Cardiac MRI** (gold standard) — best for wall thickness quantification, fibrosis (late gadolinium enhancement), and differentiation from other causes of LVH
- **Special tests:** Exercise test (to document LVOT gradient during exertion), 24-hour Holter monitor (for non-sustained VT), genetic counselling and cascade screening of first-degree relatives
- **Diagnostic criteria:** **ESC 2024 (or NICE)** — echocardiographic LV wall thickness ≥15 mm in one or more LV segments that is not explained by loading conditions; plus exclusion of other causes (aortic stenosis, hypertension, athlete’s heart). A positive genetic test confirms.
- **Never-miss test:** **12-lead ECG** in any young patient with syncope or family history of sudden death — a normal ECG almost excludes HCM (sensitivity >95% in young adults); if abnormal, proceed to echo.

### Management
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - If hypotensive **and** known HCM with LVOT obstruction: **give IV phenylephrine** (pure alpha-agonist) to raise BP without increasing contractility — do NOT give inotropes (dobutamine, adrenaline) as they worsen obstruction
  - If chest pain / dyspnoea: sit patient up, give oxygen if SpO₂ <94%, obtain IV access, ECG; avoid nitrates (reduce preload and may provoke obstruction)
  - If sustained VT or VF: synchronised cardioversion / defibrillation per ALS algorithm; amiodarone 150 mg IV over 10 min (if shocked rhythm)
- **Ward (first 24-72h):**
  - Start oral beta-blocker (e.g. bisoprolol 1.25 mg OD, titrate to heart rate 50-70 bpm) to reduce myocardial oxygen demand and LVOT gradient
  - If beta-blocker contraindicated or ineffective: verapamil SR (120–240 mg OD) — but avoid in patients with severe LVOT obstruction or heart failure with reduced EF
  - Monitor for arrhythmias (ECG telemetry), fluid balance (aim euvolemia)
  - Arrange echocardiogram within 24-48h if not done in ED
  - Review for SCD risk assessment using **HCM Risk-SCD calculator** (ESC) — requires age, septal wall thickness, left atrial size, LVOT gradient, NSVT on Holter, family history of SCD, unexplained syncope
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - First-line: beta-blocker (bisoprolol/propranolol) tolerated dose
  - If symptoms persist despite beta-blocker: add disopyramide (250–500 mg TDS) — but only under specialist supervision due to pro-arrhythmic risk
  - If marked LVOT obstruction and drug-refractory: consider septal reduction therapy (alcohol septal ablation or myectomy) — refer to specialist centre
  - All patients with HCM should have **lifestyle advice**: avoid competitive sports, high-intensity exercise, dehydration, alcohol excess
  - Evaluate for ICD if HCM Risk-SCD >6% over 5 years (ESC) or >4% (NICE TG6)
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - Fluids: of caution; avoid aggressive IV fluids unless clearly hypovolaemic (can worsen obstruction if preload is too high? Actually, hypovolaemia is worse. In HCM, maintain euvolemia; if dry, give 250–500 mL bolus of 0.9% saline slowly. If wet, restrict. **Write:** "Maintain euvolemia. Give 500 mL 0.9% NaCl IV over 1h if systolic BP <90 or signs of dehydration; otherwise monitor strict I/O."
  - VTE prophylaxis: low-molecular-weight heparin (enoxaparin 40 mg SC OD) if admitted >24h and high risk (immobility + obesity) — adjust for renal function
  - Diet: normal, but avoid large meals (can reduce preload)
  - Observations: 4-hourly NEWS2, continuous ECG telemetry if admitted for syncope/arrhythmia

### Drugs & Doses
- **Beta-blockers (first-line):**
  - Bisoprolol: start 1.25 mg PO OD, titrate up to 10 mg OD as tolerated (target HR 50-70 bpm)
  - Propranolol: 20–40 mg PO TDS, titrate to 320 mg/day in divided doses if needed
- **Non-dihydropyridine calcium channel blocker (second-line):**
  - Verapamil SR: start 120 mg PO OD, increase to 240–480 mg OD in divided doses if needed — **contraindicated if LVOT gradient >50 mmHg or EF <50%**
- **Disopyramide:** 250 mg PO TDS (max 500 mg TDS) — note: anticholinergic effects (dry mouth, urinary retention); contraindicated in glaucoma, prostatism; must have baseline ECG (prolongs QT)
- **For hypotension in acute LVOT obstruction:**
  - Phenylephrine: 100–200 mcg IV bolus, then infusion 50–200 mcg/min — titrate to systolic BP >90
  - **Do NOT use** dobutamine, adrenaline, milrinone, noradrenaline (increase contractility → worsen gradient)
- **For acute arrhythmia:**
  - Amiodarone: 150 mg IV over 10 min (for sustained VT/VF), then 300 mg IV over 1h (if recurrent)
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:** 
  - Bisoprolol: adjust in severe renal failure (eGFR <20 — start 1.25 mg OD, max 5 mg)
  - Verapamil: use with caution in hepatic impairment (reduce dose by 50%)
  - Disopyramide: dose reduction in renal failure (interval prolongation)
- **Most dangerous dose error:** **Giving dobutamine or adrenaline for hypotension in a patient with LVOT obstruction** — can cause cardiovascular collapse. Always ask "Does this patient have HCM?" before giving any IV inotrope.

### Complications
- **Acute:** 
  - Ventricular arrhythmia (VT/VF) → SCD
  - LVOT obstruction → syncope, acute pulmonary oedema
  - Atrial fibrillation → haemodynamic instability, stroke
- **Chronic:** 
  - Diastolic heart failure (with preserved EF)
  - Mitral regurgitation secondary to SAM
  - Left atrial enlargement → atrial fibrillation → stroke risk (CHA₂DS₂-VASc ≥2 → anticoagulate)
  - End-stage systolic heart failure (≤5% of patients)
  - Infective endocarditis (rare, but consider if LVOT gradient fixed)

### Prognosis
- Early diagnosis (symptomatic or high-risk) → annual mortality 1% (with modern management). However, SCD risk is highest in young, symptomatic patients with massive hypertrophy (>30 mm) or NSVT. Many patients live normal lives with symptom control; a minority progress to end-stage heart failure requiring transplant.

### Pearls (positive actions)
- **Always perform a Valsalva manoeuvre at the bedside** — if you hear an increase in the murmur, think HCM.
- **Send first-degree relatives for echocardiography screening** (cascade screening) — 50% will have a normal echo at first; repeat every 3-5 years.
- **Use the HCM Risk-SCD calculator** before discharging any newly diagnosed patient — if score >6% (ESC), discuss ICD.

### Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid)
- **Do NOT give nitrates** (GTN spray, IV nitroglycerin) for chest pain in suspected HCM — they reduce preload, worsen obstruction, and can cause syncope.
- **Do NOT use verapamil** in patients with LVOT gradient >50 mmHg or reduced EF — it can precipitate heart failure.
- **Do not ignore atrial fibrillation** — patients with HCM need lifelong anticoagulation if CHA₂DS₂-VASc ≥2, even if low score, because stroke risk is higher than in general population.

### Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home)
- **Return if:** 
  - Recurrent syncope or near-syncope (especially with exertion)
  - Sustained palpitations (rapid, irregular) — may be atrial fibrillation
  - New chest pain, breathlessness at rest, or leg swelling
- **Follow-up:** 
  - Cardiology clinic within 2-4 weeks for echo review and risk assessment
  - Family screening referral to clinical genetics
  - If ICD implanted: device clinic in 1 month

### Escalation Criteria
- **Call senior / cardiology reg immediately if:**
  - Systolic BP <90 with syncope or severe symptoms (suspect acute LVOT obstruction or VT) — give phenylephrine as above
  - Sustained VT or VF (call crash team)
  - NEWS2 ≥5 with respiratory distress (possible acute pulmonary oedema)
  - GCS <15 or seizure (syncope gone bad)
- **ICU admission:** 
  - Refractory hypotension despite phenylephrine
  - Recurrent ventricular arrhythmias
  - Need for invasive ventilation or haemodynamic support (IABP may be considered, but rarely needed)
